coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor for rapid diagnosis and early-stage detection of pancreatic cancer using Er-GQDs decorated MoS2 nanoflowers
coredata.dc:creator: Huynh, Trung Viet
Tran, Hai Linh
Thi Ngoc Anh, Nguyen
Doong, Ruey-An
coredata.dc:description: In this study, for the first time, the erbium-doped graphene quantum dots (Er-GQDs) were decorated onto a three-dimensional MoS2 nanoflower (EGM) for electrochemically sensitive and selective detection of carbohydrate antigen, CA 19-9, in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and biological fluids. The MoS2 nanoflowers (MoS2 NF) were hydrothermally prepared at 200 °C for 24 h, and then 2–8 nm of Er-GQDs were uniformly deposited onto 200 nm MoS2 NF. The MoS2 NF has a high electric conductivity for electrochemical performance, whereas Er-GQDs act as an effective anchor with anti-CA19-9 antibodies (CA19-9 Ab) for ultra-selectivity to CA19-9 antigen. The SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor has substantial benefits by providing a long-lasting, ultra-sensitive, and highly selective platform for CA19-9 antigen detection. The dynamic ranges in PBS, human serum, and saliva are 5 × 10−5–250, 5 × 10−4–250, and 3 × 10−4–250 U mL−1, respectively, with limits of detection (LODs) of (0.18–2.95) × 10−4 U mL−1. Furthermore, the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor can detect CA19-9 in human serum with a recovery of 97–108 %. The non-invasive detection of CA19-9 in saliva is stably sensitive before and after 1 h of breakfast. Moreover, the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor has excellent selectivity toward CA19-9 over the other 17 interferences. Results elaborate that the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is a promising electrochemical biosensing probe for detecting CA19-9 in human serum and saliva for clinical diagnosis and prognosis of pancreatic cancer in the early stage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Er-doped GQDs
GQD-based nanocomposites
Molybdenum dichalcogenide (MoS2) nanoflower
Pancreatic cancer
Biomedical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical property of the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor
Nanosensing of CA19-9 in PBS buffer and human serum
Non-invasive detection of CA19-9 via saliva test

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC), Taiwan for financial support under grant Nos. NSTC 112-2113-M007-031 and No. NSTC 112-2223-E-007-010.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image of (a) Er-GQDs (inset: histogram of Er-GQDs) and SEM images of (b) MoS2 NF (inset: narrowed size distribution of single flower MoS2) and (c) EGM nanocomposites, and (d) TEM and (e) HRTEM images of Er-GQDs decorated MoS2 NF nanocomposites.
The deconvoluted XPS spectra of (a) Mo 3d, (b) S 2p, (c) N 1s, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) Er 4d of Er-GQDs decorated MoS2 NF.

(a) CV curves of EGM at various scan rates of 5–200 mV s−1, (b) CV curve and (c) Nyquist plots of EGM, and (d) effect of pH on the stability of the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor before and after the addition of 250 U mL−1 anti-CA19-9.

(a) CV curves and (b) calibration curve of SPE||EGM/Ab electrode at 0–250 U mL−1 CA19-9 in PBS buffer; and (c) CV curves and (d) calibration curve of SPE||EGM/Ab at 0–250 U mL−1 CA19-9 in human serum.

The CV and calibration curves of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor at various anti CA19-9 concentrations of 0–250 U mL−1 in saliva (a) CV curves of CA19-9 before meal, (b) calibration curve of CA19-9 of before meal (c) CV curves of CA19-9 after meal for 1 h, and (d) calibration curve of CA19-9 after meal for 1 h.
The SPE||EGM/Ab Immunosensor selectivity in the presence of different interferences and anti-CA19-9.
Schematic illustration of the step-by-step fabrication of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor and the electrochemical detection of CA19-9 antigen.
Comparison of the analytical performance of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor with reported sensor for CA19-9 detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SWV: square wave voltammetry; EIS: electrochemical impedance spectroscopy; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; ECL: electrochemiluminescence (ECL); PAA: polyacrylic acid sodium salt; CS: chitosan; AuNPs@PThi: Au@polythionine composites.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor for rapid diagnosis and early-stage detection of pancreatic cancer using Er-GQDs decorated MoS2 nanoflowers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu 300044, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, 300044, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu 300044, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, 300044, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinh Long University of Technology Education, 73 Nguyen Hue Street, Vinh Long City, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vinh Long University of Technology Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vinh Long University of Technology Education, 73 Nguyen Hue Street, Vinh Long City, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, for the first time, the erbium-doped graphene quantum dots (Er-GQDs) were decorated onto a three-dimensional MoS2 nanoflower (EGM) for electrochemically sensitive and selective detection of carbohydrate antigen, CA 19-9, in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and biological fluids. The MoS2 nanoflowers (MoS2 NF) were hydrothermally prepared at 200 °C for 24 h, and then 2–8 nm of Er-GQDs were uniformly deposited onto 200 nm MoS2 NF. The MoS2 NF has a high electric conductivity for electrochemical performance, whereas Er-GQDs act as an effective anchor with anti-CA19-9 antibodies (CA19-9 Ab) for ultra-selectivity to CA19-9 antigen. The SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor has substantial benefits by providing a long-lasting, ultra-sensitive, and highly selective platform for CA19-9 antigen detection. The dynamic ranges in PBS, human serum, and saliva are 5 × 10−5–250, 5 × 10−4–250, and 3 × 10−4–250 U mL−1, respectively, with limits of detection (LODs) of (0.18–2.95) × 10−4 U mL−1. Furthermore, the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor can detect CA19-9 in human serum with a recovery of 97–108 %. The non-invasive detection of CA19-9 in saliva is stably sensitive before and after 1 h of breakfast. Moreover, the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor has excellent selectivity toward CA19-9 over the other 17 interferences. Results elaborate that the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is a promising electrochemical biosensing probe for detecting CA19-9 in human serum and saliva for clinical diagnosis and prognosis of pancreatic cancer in the early stage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pancreatic cancer has recently been identified as the third most common cause of cancer-related death. Carbohydrate antigen (CA19-9) is one of the most often used biomarkers for early-stage diagnosis of pancreatic cancer. Normally, the concentration of CA19-9 for a healthy body is ≤ 37 U mL−1 [1] and can reach 37–100 U mL−1 for an abnormal person [2,3]. The CA19-9 concentration in the body fluid of pancreatic cancer patients can be higher than 100 U mL−1 [2,3]. Moreover, CA19-9 is also a tumor marker used in saliva testing to diagnose early-stage pancreatic cancer. Flow cytometry and ELISA have been used as the standard clinical methods for detecting pancreatic cancer markers including CA19-9 [4,5]. However, the analytical procedure is tedious and time-consuming, so patients can only get the results of their CA19-9 test within a few days after sampling. Therefore, the development of sensing probes with the advantages of rapid response, low cost, non-invasive diagnosis, and early detection of pancreatic cancer is a first-ranking target to keep in demand.

Saliva is a widely available substrate that does not cause any discomfort to the patient during sample collection, which is a suitable alternative to blood sample collection for the detection of multiple pathologies, including periodontitis, oral cancers, and cardiovascular illnesses [6]. The specimen from saliva is a non-invasive collection, which is more acceptable for repeated testing. It is able to be collected not only without medical personnel but also by the patients themselves [7]. Biosensors with different detection principles, such as colorimetry, fluorescence, photoelectrochemistry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemistry, have been constructed for sensitive and selective detection of CA19-9 [8–11]. Among these methods applied, the electrochemical biosensor is one of the most proficient techniques to determine CA19-9 with high sensitivity, wide range detection, and short response time [12].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a member of the graphene family, are usually used for electrochemical applications due to their outstanding electrical conductivity, fast electron transferability, and low cost. However, the aggregation of GQDs leads to the reduction of electrical conductivity and charge-carry capability, and, therefore, doping with heteroatoms like N, S, P, and Cl, as well as combining with another nanomaterial has been developed to enhance the electrochemical performance of GQDs [13–16]. Erbium (Er) is a lanthanoid element with a stable electron saturation structure, variable oxidation states, and higher redox reaction behavior [17], making it an excellent doping candidate for GQDs [18]. Previous work has successfully fabricated Er-doped GQDs for the optical detection of ferric ions [19]. Furthermore, Er-doped GQD nanocomposite shows a high performance for asymmetric supercapacitors because of the ability to generate electrochemically active sites [20]. However, the use of Er-GQDs for electrochemical detection of CA19-9 has received less attention.

In addition to Er-doped GQDs, the use of transition metal dichalcogenide has recently received attractive attention for electrochemical biosensor applications. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a potential 2D material with exceptional thermal stability and high electrocatalytic activity, making this material useful in various applications like medical analysis, energy, and environmental science [21–23]. However, 2D sheet-like structures have drawbacks such as poor electrolyte proximity, limited surface area utilization efficiency, restacking, and difficulty in preparation [24]. 3D nanoflower (NF) structures can improve ion and electrolyte diffusion into materials by increasing surface area and improving the sensitivity of the electrochemical performance of the material. The MoS2 NF was used recently as electrode material for hydrogen evolution and supercapacitor application [25,26]. Nevertheless, the distinct S-Mo-S layered structure and the weak van der Waals forces between layers influence the electron/ion transfer, resulting in intrinsically low electronic conductivity and structural degradation [27]. Therefore, the modification of MoS2 to enhance the electrochemical performance of MoS2-based electrochemical biosensors is critical. Combining Er-GQDs with MoS2 NF is a promising strategy for effective electrochemical detection of cancer makers.

In this study, the electrochemical sensor using Er-GQDs decorated MoS2 NF (EGM) as the sensing probe was fabricated for early-stage detection of pancreatic cancer using CA 19-9 as the biomarker. As shown in Scheme 1, the Er-GQDs were prepared via a facial one-step hydrothermal reaction. Then, the EGM was hydrothermally fabricated at 200 ℃ for 4 h. EGM's morphology, microstructures, and electrochemical properties, including cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), were investigated. After immobilizing the monoclonal CA 19-9 antibody, the CA 19-9 anchored EGM was deposited onto the screen-printed electrode (SPE) for electrochemical detection of CA 19-9 in human serum and saliva. The electrochemical sensor performance of EGM exhibits excellent electrocatalytic activity and good chemical stability for CA19-9 detection in practical human serum, which indicates that the immunosensor can be applied to the analysis of real samples. Furthermore, non-invasive diagnostic detection in saliva has shown efficient clinical diagnostic and early-stage detection for pancreatic cancer. In particular, the effect of substances in saliva on CA19-9 detection is investigated via before and after breakfast testing. Results elaborate that the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is a promising electrochemical biosensing probe for the detection of CA19-9 with short response time, low cost, non-invasive diagnosis, and early detection of pancreatic cancer in clinical diagnosis, which can open an avenue to develop the GQD-decorated MoS2 nanocomposites as the biosensing probe for clinical diagnosis and biomolecular interaction studies.
The Experimental section including chemicals, synthesis of Er-GQDs@MOS2 NF, characterization, and analytical performance can be found in Supplementary materials (S1–S8).

The morphology and size distribution of Er-GQDs, MoS2 NF, and EGM nanomaterials were examined. TEM image discloses Er-GQDs with sizes ranging from 3.5 to 5 nm, averaging 4.7 nm ( Fig. 1 a), and MoS2 NF with size distribution of 150–200 nm (Fig. 1 b). The morphology of EGM nanocomposite maintains the flower-shaped of MoS2 (Fig. 1 c). Er-GQDs are successfully decorated onto the surface petal of MoS2 NF (Fig. 1 d), as confirmed by HRTEM showing lattice spacing is 0.21 nm and 0.63 nm corresponding to (100) plane of Er-GQDs and (002) lattice plane of MoS2 NF (Fig. 1 e), respectively. Additionally, the AFM image shows Er-GQDs heights in the range of 0.8–3.5 nm, suggesting contain 2–8 layers of graphene (Fig. S1). EDS and mapping results display the Er-GQDs homogenously distributed onto the petal of the MoS2 NF with elemental compositions of C, O, and Er at 60.8 %, 12.9 %, and 0.5 %, respectively (Fig. S2).

Fig. S3 shows the XPS survey scan of MoS2 NF and EGM nanocomposites. Typically, MoS2 NF exhibits Mo 3d, Mo 3p, S 2p, and N 1s peaks at 230, 412, 162, and 391 eV, respectively, with N element contributed from the L-cysteine precursor. After Er-GQDs, decoration introduces major peaks at 284, 532, and 150 eV with C 1s, O 1s, and Er 4d, respectively [19]. The Mo:S atomic ratio is 0.46 with stoichiometry of MoS2 NF [28]. Deconvolution of Mo 3d spectra ( Fig. 2a) displays a Mo(IV) oxidation state with two distinct peaks at 228.9 (Mo 3d5/2) and 232.15 eV (Mo 3d3/2) [25] and the weak peak of S 2 s is also obtained. Furthermore, the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibits two peaks with S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 binding energies for S2− [29]. The N 1s spectrum displays four peaks corresponding to Mo 3p (Mo-N), pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N (Fig. 2c) [28]. The deconvolution of C 1s of EGM nanocomposite displays CC species and C-N bond at 283.85 and 284.5 eV (Fig. 2d), respectively [19]. In addition, C-O/C-O-Mo bond can be observed at 287.27 eV [30]. The homogenous distribution of N content in MoS2 NF plays a vital role in the interconnection of MoS2 NF and Er-GQDs through forming C-N bonds [31]. In Fig. 2e, the O 1s spectra represent the C-OH, C-O/O-C-Mo, CO, and Er-O groups [19]. Moreover, the deconvoluted Er 4d displays two peaks 4 d5/2 and 4 d3/2 (Fig. 2f) [19,20].

The Raman spectra (Fig. S4a) of MoS2 NF and EGM are shown peaks at 373.7 and 406.1 cm−1 [32], with in-plane E1 2 g mode and the out-plane A1 g mode of MoS2 (MoS bonds) [28] and EGM have rich edge-terminated structures with high intensity of in-out-plane mode [21]. Furthermore, EGM displays D- and G-bands with an ID/IG ratio of 0.93, suggesting structural defects [28]. Moreover, XRD patterns (Fig. S4b ) confirm MoS2 NF crystallinity with 13.8°, 40.17°, and 59.6° 2θ and introduce Er-GQDs peaks at 28.5° 2θ [32,33].

The FTIR spectra (Fig. S4c) were recorded to elucidate functional groups, with shifts of -OH, CO, and C-O bands to 1404, 1709, and 1058 cm−1 in EGM attributed to Er-GQDs deposition. The broad OH peak centered at 3406 cm−1 presented the hydrophilic nature of the prepared materials. The absorption peaks at 620 and 1129 cm−1 are associated with Mo-S stretching vibration [24,25]. The 1044 and 1448 cm−1 peaks are attributed to C-N and anti-symmetric C-H vibrations, respectively [28]. The as-prepared Er-GQDs exhibit four major peaks at 1046, 1382, 1618, and 1698 cm−1, which can be assigned as C-O, C-OH, CC and CO, respectively. The peak located at 520 cm−1 is the footprint of the Er-O vibration bond [19]. The FTIR spectra of EGM still show all the Er-GQDs and MoS2 NF peaks, indicating the maintenance of the functional group of these two materials.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S4d) of the MoS2-NF and EGM nanocomposites can be classified as the type IV adsorption isotherms with H3-type hysteresis loop, indicating the mesoporous nature. In addition, the decoration of Er-GQDs increases the specific surface area of EGM to 45.2 m2 g−1 in comparison to that of MoS2 NF (26.3 m2 g−1) [34], which means the Er-GQDs decorated MoS2 can enhance the electrochemical performance [34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical property of the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical process is the foundation for detecting CA19-9 with the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor used [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− as an electrochemical probe to facilitate electron transfer. CA19-9 detection was recorded with CV and calculated based on the Ipa. Fig. S5 shows the CV profiles of GQDs, Er-GQDs, MoS2 NF, and EGM nanocomposites at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in a 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−. The Ipa of SPE is 0.27 mA. After coating with GQDs, Er-GQDs, MoS2 NF, and EGM nanocomposites, the Ipa values of SPE||GQDs, SPE||Er-GQDs, SPE||MoS2 NF, and SPE||EGM increase to 0.73, 1.22, 1.47 and 1.73 mA, respectively. Remarkably, the Ipa of SPE||Er-GQDs is 1.73 times higher than that of SPE||GQDs, presumably attributed to the synergistic effect from Er3+ doping to GQDs via π bonding in the framework that sustains their electron donor-acceptor [17,18]. Additionally, the Ipa of SPE||EGM is 16 % higher than that of SPE||MoS2 NF, highlighting enhanced electro-kinetic activities via the decoration of Er-GQDs onto MoS2 NF. The Ipa of SPE||EGM is 6.4 times higher than that of SPE, depicting the enhancement of electrochemically active surfaces after EGM nanocomposite coating. XPS spectra analysis after 25 CV cycles ( Fig. S6) shows a 30 % noteworthy decrease in S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 peaks for MoS2 NF. However, EGM nanocomposite only displays around a 2 % decrease, corroborating the significant contribution of composites to stabilizing the S-Mo-S structure by decorating Er-GQDs for the electrochemical performance of MoS2 NF [17,18]. It is noteworthy that the S2- 2p3/2 and S2- 2p1/2 shifts to a higher binding energy of 0.3 eV after 25 times of CV cycles in comparison with the original spectra, presumably attributed to the phase transformation of MoS2 from metallic phase (1T) MoS2 to semiconductor phase (2H) MoS2 [36–41].

The contact angle experiment was further conducted to provided more evidence on the hydrophilicity of sensing probe. As illustrated in Fig. S7a, the contact angles of MoS2 NF and EGM are 54.2° and 36.9°, respectively, corroborating the excellent hydrophilic property of the as-developed nanomaterials. The decoration of Er-GQDs provide abundant carboxylic group (-COOH), resulting in the increase in wettability of the EGM in comparison with MoS2 NF. Moreover, the -COOH group not only creates more active sites for sensing purposes via the bonding between amine groups of Ab and -COOH group of Er-GQDs but can also enhance the electrochemical performance of EGM and the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor for the detection of CA19-9.

Fig. 3a shows CV curves of the SPE||EGM at different scan rates of 5–200 mV s−1 in the potential window of − 0.3 to 0.8 V. The oxidation peak shifts from 0.22 to 0.25 V, while the reduction peak shifts from 0.14 to 0.09 V as the scan rate increases, which is attributed to the inadequate ions immigration time at high scan rates [42,43]. It is noted that diffusion will dominate the CV curve at a high scan rate, and more electrolyte ions can accumulate at the electrode-electrolyte interface, while only few ions participate to the charge transfer reaction [44]. Fig. S7b compares the CV curves of SPE||EGM in the presence and absence of target molecules at scan rates of 100 and 200 mV s⁻¹. At the high scan rate of 200 mV s⁻¹, the ΔIpa decreases 0.9 mA after the addition of CA 19-9, while only 0.6 mA decrease in ΔIpa is observed at 100 mV s−1. This indicates that the electrode-electrolyte interface reaction may not have sufficient time to reach equilibrium at 200 mV s−1. Consequently, the scan rate of 100 mV s−1 was used for the detection of CA19-9. Fig. 3b illustrates CV curves with stepwise electrode modification, indicating immobilization of poly-L lysine, anti-CA19-9 antibody, BSA, and CA19-9 antigen. The SPE||EGM demonstrates an impressive electrochemical performance with an Ipa of 1.73 mA. After coating poly-L- lysine and anti-CA19-9 antibody, decreased Ipa to 1.11, 1.06, and 1.02 mA, respectively, signifying successful linking anti-CA19-9 antibody and CA19-9 via H-bond with reduced the Ipa to 0.19 mA [43,45].

The EIS was used to investigate the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the SPE||EGM biosensor. Fig. S7c shows the Nyquist plot of the SPE||EGM biosensor, discloses a decrease compared SPE||MoS2 NF with Rct from 244 Ω to 181 Ω (Er-GQDs decoration), suggesting conductivity enhancement. Moreover, Fig. 3c displays the change in impedance during the step-by-step preparation of the SPE||EGM biosensor. After using PL as a cross-linker to bind the anti-CA19-9 antibody to the SPE||EGM surface, the carboxyl-amine interaction increases the Rct value to 304 Ω. When Ab and BSA are added to the electrode, the impedance rises from 424 Ω to 766 Ω. Furthermore, the association of CA19-9 with anti-CA19-9 antibody significantly increases impedance to 1236 Ω, indicating effective detection.

The pH effect has a significant impact on the stability of detection performance. Fig. 3d depicts the pH effect on SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor, demonstrating an optimal detection performance at 7.5. The Ipa increases from 1.1 to 1.6 mA as the pH increases from 5 to 7.5 without CA19-9. Further increase in pH reduces the Ipa due to leaching of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− into the aqueous solution at high pH conditions. Because the OH⁻ can directly enter the microporous structure of the electrode and react with [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− [46]. In the presence of CA19-9, the Ipa intensities decrease to 0.6, 0.32, and 0.21 mA at pH 5, pH 7, and pH 8, respectively, asserting optimal CA19-9 detection at pH 7.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanosensing of CA19-9 in PBS buffer and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: After the successful fabrication of the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor under the optimization conditions, the electrochemical performance of the electrode on CA19-9 detection was further evaluated with 5 µL drop-casting of EGM on SPE (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. S9 , the Ipa changes in the presence and absence of 250 U mL−1 CA19-9. The unchangeability in Ipa is observed at the SPE||Ab electrode. The result is considered unable to stand CA19-9 in the SPE surface, which is an impossible adsorption behavior for antigen-antibody interaction. Moreover, the analytical performance of CA19-9 detection by SPE||Er-GQDs/Ab, SPE||MoS2 NF/Ab, and SPE||EGM/Ab with a decrease of Ipa at 27 %, 62 %, and 90 %, respectively, corroborating that the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor can effectively detect CA19-9. The homogenous dispersion of Er-GQDs on the well-defined 3D of MoS2 NF is beneficial for electron transfer and increases the active site for electrochemical detection. Adding Er-GQDs also improves surface area to bind Ab via amide bonding between the amine groups of Ab and the carboxyl groups of Er-GQDs [47].

Fig. 4a displays the CV curves of various concentrations of CA19-9. The redox current intensity decreases as CA19-9 concentration increases from 0 to 250 U mL−1 because of the irreversible process in electrochemistry [48]. This result is also a typical adsorption behavior for antigen-antibody interaction in immunosensors, which is considered a sluggish charge transfer of CA19-9 to SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor. Fig. 4b depicts the linear relationship between CA19-9 and the Ipa ratio. An inverse linear relationship in the CA19-9 concentration range of 5 × 10−5–250 U mL−1 is obtained. A good linear relationship between log concentration (log C) and Ipa ratio with the equation of Y = − 0.12 log C + 0.47 and an excellent correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99 is observed. The practical applicability of the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is investigated further by using human serum as the matrix. The human serum sample was diluted 10 times with PBS containing [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− at pH 7.5, and then various CA19-9 concentrations ranging from 5 × 10−5 to 250 U mL−1 were added to the human serum samples. It is noteworthy that the use of biological fluids from patients as the matrix is conducive to validate the real application of the developed immunosensor. Although the as-developed sensing probe cannot be used to detect CA 19-9 in human serum and saliva from patients in this study because of the constrain of the regulation of the Institutional Review Board (IRB) and hospital ethical guidelines, the superior analytical performance of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor reveals the feasibility of using the developed immunosensor for CA 19-9 detection in real patient samples.

As shown in Fig. 4c, the Ipa intensity decreases as the concentration of CA19-9 increases from 0 to 250 U mL−1. In addition, the relationship between the Ipa ratio and CA19-9 concentration follows the linear equations of Y = - 0.11 log C + 0.48 with a good correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.98. A dynamic range from 5 × 10−4–250 U mL−1 is also obtained (Fig. 4d). The excellent analytical performance of the SPE||EGM immunosensor in diluted human serums indicates the superior applicability of the developed immunosensor in real samples. The detection limit (LOD) of CA19-9 is calculated to be 0.18 × 10−4 U mL−1 in PBS and 2.95 × 10−4 U mL−1 in diluted human serums. The wide dynamic range with low LOD indicates that the developed immunosensor is quite suitable for the early detection of pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the early stage [2]. A previous study combined Au nanoflower with 2D red phosphorus nanoplates to developed the SERS-based immunosensor for the detection of CA 19-9, and a LOD of 0.74 × 10−4 U mL−1 was achieved [10]. Moreover, several electrochemical sensors have also been developed for CA 19-9 detection. Table 1 summarizes the electrochemical biosensor for CA19-9 detection using different sensing techniques. Several electrochemical methods including differential pulse voltammetry, square wave voltammetry, EIS, and electrochemiluminescence with nanomaterials like AuPt nanocrystals, Au@polythionine, and multi-wall CNT@Fe3O4 as the sensing probes have developed for CA19-9 detection [8,49–54]. The linear range of 5 × 10−4–520 U mL−1 with LOD from 1.6 × 10−3 to 0.26 U mL−1 were obtained. In this study, the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor exhibits a wide dynamic range (5 × 10−5–250 U mL−1) and low LOD values of (0.18–2.95) × 10−4 U mL−1 in comparison with those reported results. Moreover, the analytical performance obtained in this study is better than those of Thermo Fisher ELISA kit (LOD 0.3 U mL−1) and immunochromatographic assay which the linear range and LOD were 5–100 U mL−1 and 5 U mL−1, respectively [55]. These results elaborate the superiority of the as-developed immunosensor for the rapid and sensitive detection of CA19-9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-invasive detection of CA19-9 via saliva test
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows CA19-9 detection via SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor in saliva as a non-invasive diagnostic. Furthermore, the effects of the as-developed system on CA19-9 detection in saliva were studied before and after 1 h of breakfast. As shown in Fig. 5a, the current in CV curves rapidly decreases, and an excellent linear equation of Y = − 0.12 log C + 0.45 with a good correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99 and dynamic range of 3 × 10−4–250 U mL−1 is obtained (Fig. 5b).

The influence of the digestive system on CA19-9 detection in saliva was further studied 1 h after breakfast, and the result was compared to before breakfast. As shown in Fig. 5c, the CV curves decrease rapidly compared to before breakfast. Interestingly, the detection trend of CV curves is similar to before breakfast. Another good relationship between Ipa and CA19-9 concentration is observed after 1 h after breakfast. The meal experiment follows the linear equation of Y = − 0.1 log C + 0.57 with a good correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.99 and a dynamic range of 3 × 10−4–250 U mL−1 (Fig. 5d). The LODs before and after breakfast are similar (1.041 × 10−4 and 1.042 × 10−4 U mL−1), indicating that the detection of CA19-9 from the saliva is relatively stable. Foods have a negligible influence on detection performance. Furthermore, the recovery of different concentrations of CA19-9 in human serum and saliva samples using the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is summarized in Table S1. The recovery of low concentrations of CA19-9 (3 × 10−4–1 × 10−2 U mL−1) ranges from 97 % to 106 %. As the CA19-9 concentration increases to medium (1–37 U mL−1) and high (100–250 U mL−1) concentrations, the recoveries still maintain in the range of 97–102 % and 99–101 %, respectively. This result indicates that the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor has superior analytical performance and excellent reliability for CA19-9 detection in biological matrix environments.

Fig. 6 shows the selectivity of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor for CA 19-9 via different interferences, including 5 of protein cancer, 1 enzyme, 6 amino acids, and 5 ions. The selectivity of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor with CA19-9 decreases by 95 % of Ipa, demonstrating its high selectivity for CA19-9. Despite alpha-amylase, the saliva gland enzyme causes a slight 4 % decrease in Ipa, consistent with SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor detection in saliva testing. Furthermore, Fe3+ and Fe2+ metal ions enhance the Ipa ratio of SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor, which is the natural conductivity from metal properties.

In this study, we have successfully synthesized an ultrasensitive SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor for electrochemical detection of CA19-9 in different media, including PBS, human serum, and saliva. The 3-D MoS2 was fabricated in a flower-shaped shape by hydrothermal method, and then small particles of Er-GQDs were decorated on the petal of MoS2 NF to form 150 to 200 nm EGM. The well-defined 3D MoS2 NF supports the homogeneous deposition of Er-GQDs to improve the conductivity for electron transfer. Moreover, the existence of Er-GQDs inhibits the sulfur corrosion from S-Mo-S relaxed layer of MoS2 NF, which can maintain the electrochemical performance of EGM. Furthermore, the Er-GQDs improve the binding affinity of Ab via an amide bonding between the -NH2 of Ab and the COOH of Er-GQDs. The SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is stable and biocompatible, which shows a high current response with exceptional electrocatalytic performance for CA19-9 detection. The developed SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor has a dynamic range of 5 × 10−5–250 U mL−1 and an extremely low LOD of 0.18 × 10−4 and 2.95 × 10−4 U mL−1 in PBS buffer and human serum, respectively. The non-invasive detection of CA19-9, evidenced in saliva testing, exhibits a low LOD of 1.04 × 10−4 U mL−1 before and after the 1-hour breakfast experiment. The saliva testing results show unaffected CA19-9 detection from food and beverages. It is also highly selective over 17 different interfering biomarkers and proteins. Furthermore, the SPE||EGM immunosensor has excellent biosensing performance for CA19-9 detection in human serum and saliva with recovery of 97–108 %. These findings elaborate that the SPE||EGM/Ab immunosensor is superior for ultra-highly sensitive and selective biosensing of CA19-9, which is considered an early-stage diagnostic and prognostic for pancreatic cancer and other cancer markers.

Trung Viet Huynh: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ruey-An Doong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Linh Hai Tran: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Nguyen Anh: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Software, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC), Taiwan for financial support under Grant nos. NSTC 112-2113-M007-031 and NSTC 112-2223-E-007-010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135893.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huynh Trung Viet received his master degree from National Tsing Hua University in 2020 under the supervision of professor Ruey-An Dong. He is now a PhD candidate in the Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan. His research work mainly focuses on the fabrication of environmentally benign carbon based nanocomposites for optical and electrochemical nanosensing applications.

Hai Linh Tran received her PhD degree in Biomedical Engineering and Environmental Sciences from National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu, Taiwan in 2021 under the supervision of Professor Ruey-an Doong. Her research focuses on the fabrication of GQD-based nanocomposites for optical and electrochemical nano-sensing applications. She is currently serving as a postdoctoral researcher in the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, School of Medicine, Tulane University, Louisiana, USA.

Nguyen Thi Ngoc Anh received her master and Ph.D. degrees from Can Tho University, Vietnam and National Chiao Tung University, Taiwan, respectively. She is currently a lecturer of Vinh Long University of Technology Education, Vietnam. Her research interest lies in the development of smart materials for agriculture and environmental applications.

Ruey-An Doong is a chair professor in the Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Taiwan. His current research interest includes the fabrication of nanomaterials with novel optical and electrochemical properties for biosensing of analytes, environmentally benign nanotechnology for treatment of contaminants, and porous materials for energy storage and conversion.
